relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: The messengers entered Sodom at evening, and Lot, sitting in the gate, rose to meet them.
Lot urged them into shelter, knowing the city square was not safe for strangers.
He pressed them strongly, made a meal, and brought them under his roof.
Before rest could come, the city surrounded the house from young to old.
The mob demanded the visitors, turning desire into domination and threat.
Lot went out and shut the door behind him, standing between the mob and his guests.
He pleaded with them not to do harm.
Lot’s attempt at protection was morally broken, exposing how deeply patriarchy and violence had distorted the city and his own judgment.
The mob turned on Lot, rejecting the sojourner who dared to judge them.
The messengers pulled Lot back inside and shut the door.
Blindness fell on the violent crowd, and they wore themselves out searching for the entrance.
Lot was told to gather anyone he had, because the place was about to be ruined.
The outcry had risen before the Eternal, and the messengers had come because violence had filled the city.
Lot warned his sons-in-law, but they heard him as if he were joking.
At dawn, the messengers urged him to take his family and flee before the city’s iniquity consumed them.
Lot lingered, so mercy seized his hand and brought him out.
They told him to escape with his life and not look back.
Lot pleaded, unable to trust the mountain path.
He named the kindness he had received, yet still feared the harm might cling to him.
He asked to flee to a small city, asking for a smaller mercy he could bear.
His request was granted, and the small city was spared for his escape.
He was told to hurry, for judgment would wait until he arrived.
The sun rose as Lot entered Zoar.
Then sulfur and fire fell upon Sodom and Gomorrah from the heavens.
The cities, plain, inhabitants, and growth of the ground were overturned.
Lot’s woman looked back and became a pillar of salt, frozen in the backward gaze.
Abraham rose early and returned to the place where he had stood in intercession.
He looked toward the plain and saw smoke rising like a furnace.
In the overthrow, the Divine remembered Abraham and sent Lot out from the ruin.
Lot left Zoar in fear and dwelled in a cave with his daughters.
The daughters believed the future had collapsed and that no path of ordinary continuation remained.
Out of fear for lineage and survival, they formed a disturbing plan involving wine and their father.
The firstborn carried out the plan while Lot remained unaware.
The next day, the plan was repeated for the younger daughter.
Again wine covered awareness, and the younger completed the plan.
Both daughters conceived from the cave’s broken aftermath.
From the firstborn came Moab, a nation born from trauma and survival.
From the younger came Ben-Ammi, father of Ammon, another lineage from the ruins.
